---
title: "Parallel Patterns in Programming"
subtitle: "CX3, modules, PBS, OpenMP, MPI"
author: "Gerard Gorman"
institute: "Department of Earth Science and Engineering — Imperial College London"
date: today
date-format: long
format:
  beamer:
    aspectratio: 169
    theme: Madrid
    colortheme: seahorse
    fonttheme: professionalfonts
    pdf-engine: lualatex
    highlight-style: github
    code-block-font-size: \scriptsize
    slide-level: 2
    toc: false
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        % Show only the section title on \section divider frames
        % (suppress Madrid's default "Section N" line).
        \setbeamertemplate{section page}{%
          \begin{centering}
            \begin{beamercolorbox}[sep=12pt,center]{part title}
              \usebeamerfont{section title}\insertsectionhead\par
            \end{beamercolorbox}
          \end{centering}
        }
  revealjs:
    theme: simple
    slide-number: c/t
    incremental: false
    code-overflow: wrap
    code-block-height: 600px
    highlight-style: github
    smaller: true
    controls: true
---

## Three weeks of parallel programming

**This week — HPC intro & OpenMP** (Mon 27 Apr – Fri 1 May)

- **Today:** Introduction to HPC on CX3 — modules, PBS, plus illustrative OpenMP and MPI examples (this deck).
- Rest of the week: OpenMP in depth — parallel regions, data sharing, work sharing, reductions.
- **OpenMP assessment due 16:00 Friday.**

**Week 2 — MPI** (Mon 4 May – Fri 8 May)

- Distributed-memory parallelism with MPI.
- Lecturer: **Prof Stephen Neethling**.

**Week 3 — MPI continued** (Mon 11 May – Fri 15 May)

- Further MPI topics and assessment.
- Lecturer: **Prof Stephen Neethling**.

## Today's session

A whirlwind tour so you can build, submit, and parallelise on CX3 by the end of the afternoon.

1. Access to **CX3** — login, filesystems, environment **modules**.
2. **PBS** job submission — script anatomy, queues, monitoring.
3. The running example: $\pi$ by numerical integration.
4. **OpenMP** — shared-memory parallelism (illustrative).
5. **MPI** — distributed-memory parallelism (illustrative).
6. Where to go next — pointers into this week's OpenMP work.

The OpenMP and MPI material today is illustrative — we'll go much deeper into OpenMP for the rest of the week, and Prof Neethling will cover MPI properly in weeks 2–3.

## Learning outcomes

By the end of this session you will be able to:

- Log in to the Imperial **CX3** HPC cluster.
- Load the right **environment modules** for a parallel C++ build.
- Write, submit, and monitor a **PBS** job script.
- Parallelise a simple serial C++ program with **OpenMP** and run it on CX3.
- Parallelise the same program with **MPI** and run it on CX3.

No prior HPC experience assumed; you should be comfortable with C++ basics, a command line, and a text editor.

## Our running example — $\pi$ by numerical integration

$$
\pi \;\approx\; \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\frac{4}{1 + \left(\frac{i-\tfrac{1}{2}}{N}\right)^2}
$$

- Embarrassingly parallel: every term is independent.
- Accuracy improves with $N$; we will use $N = 10^9$.
- We will implement it in three forms: serial → OpenMP → MPI.
- Same algorithm, same answer. The parallelisation is all in how we split the sum.

# Access to CX3

## What is CX3?

- Imperial College's general-purpose HPC cluster (CX3 = *compute cluster 3*).
- Roughly 600 compute nodes + a handful of login nodes.
- Two CPU architectures mixed in one cluster:
  - **AMD EPYC 7742** (Rome) — 128 cores / node.
  - **Intel Xeon 8358** (Ice Lake) — 64 cores / node.
- One shared parallel filesystem (RDS) visible from every node.
- Job scheduler: **PBS Professional**.

::: {.callout-tip title="Deep dive: Imperial College Research Computing Services"}
* [https://www.imperial.ac.uk/admin-services/ict/self-service/research-support/rcs/service-offering/hpc/](https://www.imperial.ac.uk/admin-services/ict/self-service/research-support/rcs/service-offering/hpc/)
* [https://icl-rcs-user-guide.readthedocs.io/en/latest/hpc/cluster-specification/](https://icl-rcs-user-guide.readthedocs.io/en/latest/hpc/cluster-specification/)
:::

## Shared resource, strict rules

- Login nodes are **shared** with everyone else — no heavy work there.
- Compute nodes are **allocated** by the scheduler; you cannot ssh to them directly.
- The right path is always: *log in → write a PBS script → submit with `qsub`*.
- Long, memory-intensive, or multi-threaded work on a login node **will be killed** by admins.

::: {.callout-tip title="Deep dive: PBS"}
* [https://www.openpbs.org/](https://www.openpbs.org/)
* SLURM is a common alternative found on many HPC systems - [https://github.com/schedmd/slurm](https://github.com/schedmd/slurm)
:::

## Connecting: plain ssh

```bash
ssh myusername@login.cx3.hpc.ic.ac.uk
```

- `login.cx3.hpc.ic.ac.uk` is a **load balancer** over four login shards.
- You will be prompted for your **Imperial College password** (the same one you use for email / Outlook).
- First connection shows a host-key fingerprint; accept it (`yes`).

## Authentication: password only

- Public-key authentication is **disabled** on RCS login nodes.
- `ssh-copy-id` will run without error but the next `ssh` still prompts for a password.
- Use `ssh` multiplexing (`ControlMaster`) if you hate typing the password repeatedly:

```bash
# ~/.ssh/config
Host login.cx3.hpc.ic.ac.uk
    ControlMaster auto
    ControlPath  ~/.ssh/cm-%r@%h:%p
    ControlPersist 1h
```

## Before you connect — network

- **On campus:** connect to `Imperial-WPA` wifi, then ssh works directly.
- **Off campus:** use Imperial's **Zscaler / Unified Access** VPN; otherwise the cluster is unreachable from the open internet.
- Eduroam on campus also works.
- If you see `ssh: connect to host …: Network is unreachable`, it's a VPN/wifi problem, not the cluster.

## Login shards and CPU arch

| Shard | CPU | Why you'd care |
|---|---|---|
| `login-b`, `login-c` | AMD Rome | Build-host for Rome compute nodes |
| `login-ai`, `login-bi` | Intel Ice Lake | Build-host for Ice Lake compute nodes |

- The load balancer picks one of them at random — you may land on Rome today and Ice Lake tomorrow.
- For this course it doesn't matter: GCC's binary-portable defaults work on both.
- If you later do performance work with `-march=native`, pin yourself to a specific shard by name.
- The **compute nodes** are also a mix of Rome and Ice Lake — we'll come back to this when we pick a node for our benchmark (see "Pinning the host class").

## The shared filesystem (RDS)

| Env var | Path | Quota | Retention |
|---|---|---|---|
| `$HOME` | `/rds/general/user/$USER/home` | 1 TB | permanent |
| `$EPHEMERAL` | `/rds/general/user/$USER/ephemeral` | 10 TB | **wiped after 30 days** |

- `$HOME` is visible from every login node *and* every compute node — same files everywhere.
- `$EPHEMERAL` is for big intermediate results you don't want to count against your home quota.
- Check your usage: `quota -s`.

## Local scratch on the compute node

- `$TMPDIR` exists only **while your job is running**.
- Lives on the compute node's fast local disk.
- Use it for I/O-heavy workloads; stage files in at the start of the job, copy results out at the end.
- For this course's examples we are CPU-bound, not I/O-bound, so we'll ignore `$TMPDIR`.

# Environment modules

## Why modules?

- A cluster has **hundreds** of compilers, libraries, MPIs, Python versions…
- They can't all be on your `$PATH` at once — they would clash.
- Solution: **environment modules** (Lmod). Load only what you need, per session.
- Think of `module load` as "put this software on my PATH for now".

## Lmod basics

`ml` is a short alias for `module`. Use either.

| Command | What it does |
|---|---|
| `ml av` | list every module currently visible |
| `ml ov` | one-line overview |
| `ml spider <keyword>` | search across **all** modules (even hidden ones) |
| `ml <name>` | load a module |
| `ml unload <name>` | unload one |
| `ml purge` | unload *everything* |
| `ml` | list what's loaded |

## Finding the right module

```bash
# Is there an MPI?  Search broadly.
$ ml spider mpi

# What versions of GCC are installed?
$ ml spider GCC
```

- `ml spider` finds modules even when they depend on another module (`tools/prod`) that is not loaded.
- `ml av` only shows the ones ready to load *right now*.

## `tools/prod` — the gateway

```bash
$ ml tools/prod
```

- Required to make the **full software catalogue** visible.
- Every `SciPy-bundle`, `R-bundle-ICL`, newer GCC, etc. lives behind this switch.
- Load it first, then load whatever else you need.

## Compiler + MPI for this course

```bash
# OpenMP (just a compiler):
ml tools/prod
ml GCC

# MPI (compiler + MPI library):
ml tools/prod
ml GCC OpenMPI
```

- `GCC` pulls a modern `g++` onto your PATH (has `-fopenmp` baked in).
- `GCC OpenMPI` additionally gives you `mpicxx`, `mpiexec`, and the MPI headers.
- **`mpirun` / `mpiexec` are not on the default PATH** until you load an MPI.

## Swapping versions

```bash
$ ml                          # what is loaded
$ ml switch GCC/13.3.0 GCC/14.2.0
$ ml                          # confirm
```

- Safer than `unload` + `load` because Lmod checks dependencies for you.

## Purging and starting fresh

```bash
$ ml purge
$ ml                          # No modules loaded
$ ml tools/prod GCC OpenMPI
```

- Purge at the top of a PBS script if you want a known-clean environment.

## Don't bake modules into `.bashrc`

- Tempting: `ml GCC` in `~/.bashrc` so it's always there.
- **Don't.** Different projects need different combinations; login-node `.bashrc` conflicts will bite you inside PBS jobs.
- Load modules explicitly **in each PBS script** — reproducible and self-documenting.

## Typical combos you'll see

- `ml tools/prod GCC` — serial C/C++ with `g++ -O3`.
- `ml tools/prod GCC CMake` — add CMake for multi-target builds.
- `ml tools/prod GCC OpenMPI` — MPI C/C++.
- `ml tools/prod GCC OpenMPI CMake` — our examples in this course.

# PBS — the job scheduler

## First rule: no heavy work on login nodes

- The MOTD on login says so. Admins enforce it.
- "Heavy" = uses multiple cores for more than a minute, or more than ~4 GB memory, or hits the disk hard.
- Why: 200 students all compiling at once on the same login node = nobody gets anything done.
- The correct channel is **always** PBS.

## The resource manager

PBS Pro does five things:

1. Tracks which compute nodes are free.
2. Sorts incoming requests into **queues** by size/time.
3. Starts your job when matching resources become free.
4. Monitors it while it runs.
5. Captures stdout/stderr into log files.

Your half of the contract: tell PBS **exactly** what you need — cores, memory, walltime.

## Anatomy of a job script

```bash
#!/bin/bash
#PBS -N my_job
#PBS -l walltime=00:30:00
#PBS -l select=1:ncpus=8:mem=8gb

cd $PBS_O_WORKDIR

ml tools/prod
ml GCC

./my_program
```

A PBS script is a normal bash script plus a block of `#PBS` comments at the top.

## The key `#PBS` directives

| Directive | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `-N jobname` | Name shown in `qstat` |
| `-l walltime=HH:MM:SS` | Max wall-clock time before PBS kills the job |
| `-l select=N:ncpus=C:mem=Xgb` | N *chunks* (≈ nodes), each with C CPUs and X GB RAM |
| `-l select=N:…:ompthreads=C` | Extra: set `OMP_NUM_THREADS` inside the job |
| `-l select=N:…:mpiprocs=C` | Extra: number of MPI ranks per chunk |
| `-o path`, `-e path` | Redirect stdout/stderr log files |

## Requesting resources — examples

* Single serial program, 1 core, 4 GB, up to 30 min:
```bash
#PBS -l walltime=00:30:00
#PBS -l select=1:ncpus=1:mem=4gb:cpu_type=rome
```

* OpenMP on one node, 8 threads, 8 GB, up to 1 h:
```bash
#PBS -l walltime=01:00:00
#PBS -l select=1:ncpus=8:mem=8gb:ompthreads=8:cpu_type=rome
```

* MPI, 16 ranks on one node:
```bash
#PBS -l walltime=01:00:00
#PBS -l select=1:ncpus=16:mem=8gb:mpiprocs=16:cpu_type=rome
```

::: {.callout-warning}
Under-request and your job is killed. Over-request and it queues for longer.
:::

## Pinning the host class — `cpu_type`

CX3 compute nodes come in two flavours:

| `cpu_type` | Hardware | Cores / node |
|---|---|---:|
| `rome` | AMD EPYC 7742 (Zen2) | 128 |
| `icelake` | Intel Xeon Platinum 8358 | 64 |

- Without `cpu_type=...`, the scheduler picks whichever is free. Two runs of the *same* benchmark can land on different hardware and differ by ~15–20 % — not a real signal, just noise.
- **For this course we pin `cpu_type=rome`** — there are ~300 rome nodes vs ~80 icelake, so queues start quickly even with a full class submitting at once.
- Swap to `cpu_type=icelake` only if you specifically want the Intel tier.

## `pbsnodes` - list all nodes and their status

See what's free right now:

```bash
pbsnodes -a | awk '/^$/{f=0} /state = free/{f=1} \
                   /cpu_type/ && f {print $3; f=0}' | sort | uniq -c
```

## `$PBS_O_WORKDIR` — the CWD trap

- When your job starts, its **current directory is `$HOME`**, not the directory you submitted from.
- This breaks `./my_program input.dat` because `input.dat` isn't in `$HOME`.
- Every PBS script should start with:

```bash
cd $PBS_O_WORKDIR
```

`$PBS_O_WORKDIR` is set by PBS to the directory you ran `qsub` in.

## Log files — read them

After `qsub my_job.pbs`:

- `my_job.o<jobid>` — stdout + PBS summary (start/end time, CPU/mem used).
- `my_job.e<jobid>` — stderr.

**Read them even when everything looks fine.** Silent failures, out-of-memory kills, and wrong-answer bugs usually announce themselves here.

## Submit, monitor, cancel

```bash
$ qsub my_job.pbs
7315932.pbs-7

$ qstat -u $USER              # your jobs
$ qstat 7315932.pbs-7         # one job

$ qdel 7315932.pbs-7          # cancel
```

Columns to watch: `S` (state), `Req'd Memory`, `Req'd Time`, `Elap Time`.

## Job states (the `S` column)

| State | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `Q` | Queued, waiting for resources |
| `R` | Running |
| `H` | Held (admin or dependency) |
| `E` | Exiting (finishing up) |
| `F` | Finished |

If a job is stuck in `Q` for hours, either the cluster is busy or your resource ask is too large.

## Interactive jobs

```bash
$ qsub -I -l select=1:ncpus=2:mem=8gb -l walltime=02:00:00
qsub: waiting for job … to start
qsub: job … ready

[user@cx3-… ~]$
```

- Gives you a live bash shell on a compute node.
- Perfect for debugging, trying commands interactively, small compile jobs.
- Still counts against your quota; still has a walltime.

## Queue picks for this course

| Demo | Queue you'll land in | Tip |
|---|---|---|
| Serial Pi | `v1_small24` / `v1_small72` | Fine; small-queue traffic is fast for short jobs |
| OpenMP on 1 node (≤64c) | `v1_medium72` | Moves quickly |
| MPI on 1 node (16–64c) | `v1_medium72` | Avoid capability queues until needed |
| Interactive shell | `v1_interactive` | Usually empty |

You don't pick the queue yourself — PBS routes based on your `-l` line.

## Common mistakes

- **Missing `cd $PBS_O_WORKDIR`** → "file not found" at runtime.
- **Missing `ml GCC`** → `g++: command not found`.
- **`mpiexec` without `ml OpenMPI`** → `mpiexec: command not found`.
- **Missing or mismatched `ompthreads` vs `ncpus`** → wrong thread count; wastes cores or over-subscribes.
- **Over-optimistic walltime** → job killed mid-run.

## What speedup should be expect?

**Amdahl's law**: if fraction $B$ of the program is serial, the speedup with $N$ cores is

$$
\mathrm{Speedup}(N) = \frac{1}{B + (1-B)/N}
$$

::: {.callout-note icon=false}
If 10 % of your code is serial, max speedup is **10×** — even with infinite cores.
:::

- To be worth the effort of parallelization, the parallel fraction has to dominate.
- Pi by integration is *embarrassingly parallel* — every term in the sum is independent, so $B \approx 0$. A best-case scenario - most cases will have some dependencies between steps which force some serialization. 

## Hands-on — submit the serial Pi

```bash
ssh myusername@login.cx3.hpc.ic.ac.uk
git clone <this-repo> ic-hpc-intro
cd ic-hpc-intro/examples

ml tools/prod GCC CMake
cmake -B build -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release
cmake --build build -j

qsub serial/pi_serial.pbs
qstat -u $USER
# wait until state = F, then:
cat pi_serial.o*
```

Goal: see `pi=3.141592653…` and the wall-time of ~10 s for $N = 10^9$.

# OpenMP on CX3

## Shared memory parallelism

- OpenMP parallelises a program **within one compute node**.
- All threads share the same address space — they can read and write the same variables.
- Pros:
  - Often straightforward to get *satisfactory* speedup
  - Portable.
- Cons:
  - Shared memory systems only (ie single node).
  - Functional portability $\ne$ performance portable.

## OpenMP directive: `parallel`

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <omp.h>

int main() {
    #pragma omp parallel
    {
        std::cout << "Hello from thread " << omp_get_thread_num()
                  << " of " << omp_get_num_threads() << '\n';
    }
}
```

- Every line inside the `{ … }` runs on every thread.
- Number of threads comes from `OMP_NUM_THREADS` at runtime.

## The fork-join model

```
  [master thread running serially]
    #pragma omp parallel  <-- fork N threads
    { 
    |---+---+---+---+ 
    |   |   |   |   |
    |   |   |   |   |     <-- do work in parallel
    |   |   |   |   |
    |---+---+---+---+
    }                     <-- join back to master
  [master continues serially]
```

- Serial code runs on one (master) thread.
- A `#pragma omp parallel` region forks a team.

## OpenMP directive: `for`

```cpp
#pragma omp parallel for
for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
    a[i] = b[i] + c[i];
}
```

- OpenMP divides the iterations of the loop across the team.
- No explicit index-juggling; each thread gets a chunk of `i`.
- Combines `parallel` + `for` into one directive (common shortcut).

## OpenMP clause: `reduction`

Summing across threads naïvely has a **data race** on the accumulator. Fix:

```cpp
double sum = 0.0;
#pragma omp parallel for reduction(+:sum)
for (long long i = 1; i <= n; ++i) {
    sum += f(i);
}
```

- Every thread gets its own private `sum` variable; `+` operator combines them into the master thread.
- OpenMP `reduction` supports operators `+ * & | && || min max` and others.

## Variable scoping

| Clause | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `shared(x)` | all threads see the same `x` (default for globals) |
| `private(x)` | each thread gets its own uninitialised copy |
| `firstprivate(x)` | each thread gets its own copy, initialised from the master's value |
| `reduction(op:x)` | private + combined at the end |

::: {.callout-warning}
Default is shared. A **race condition** is when two threads read-modify-write the same variable without coordination: updates get lost and the result depends on thread timing, so the program is non-deterministic (works once, fails the next run). Race conditions typically happen when you forget a variable should have been private.
:::

## Pi — serial reference

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <iomanip>
int main() {
    const long long n = 1'000'000'000LL;
    const double w = 1.0 / static_cast<double>(n);
    double sum = 0.0;
    for (long long i = 1; i <= n; ++i) {
        const double x = w * (static_cast<double>(i) - 0.5);
        sum += 4.0 / (1.0 + x * x);
    }
    const double pi = w * sum;
    std::cout << std::setprecision(15) << "pi=" << pi << '\n';
}
```

## Pi — OpenMP version

```cpp
 // ...
#include <omp.h>

int main() {
    // ...

    #pragma omp parallel for reduction(+:sum)           // <-- only change
    for (long long i = 1; i <= n; ++i) {
        const double x = w * (static_cast<double>(i) - 0.5);
        sum += 4.0 / (1.0 + x * x);
    }

    // ...
}
```

## Building — plain `g++` or CMake

Command-line:

```bash
g++ -O3 -fopenmp pi_openmp.cpp -o pi_openmp
```

CMake (what the repo uses):

```cmake
find_package(OpenMP REQUIRED COMPONENTS CXX)
add_executable(pi_openmp pi_openmp.cpp)
target_link_libraries(pi_openmp PRIVATE OpenMP::OpenMP_CXX)
```

`find_package(OpenMP REQUIRED)` pulls in the right `-fopenmp` flag for your compiler, and fails if OpenMP is missing.

## PBS for OpenMP — `ompthreads`

```bash
#!/bin/bash
#PBS -N pi_openmp
#PBS -l walltime=00:10:00
#PBS -l select=1:ncpus=8:mem=4gb:ompthreads=8:cpu_type=rome

cd $PBS_O_WORKDIR
ml tools/prod
ml GCC OpenMPI        # OpenMPI also brings lstopo/numactl onto PATH
./build/openmp/pi_openmp
```

- `ncpus=8` — give me 8 cores on one node.
- `ompthreads=8` — set `OMP_NUM_THREADS=8` inside the job.
- **Default:** (`ompthreads = ncpus`) — one thread per allocated core.
- `cpu_type=rome` — ensures you always gets the same kind of node.


## What hardware did PBS give you?

Three commands, run **inside the PBS job**.

```bash
lscpu                      # CPU model, sockets, cores, cache sizes
numactl --hardware         # NUMA nodes + distance matrix
lstopo-no-graphics --no-io # full hierarchy: socket → NUMA → cache → core
```

- `lscpu` is always on `PATH`. The other two live in `hwloc` / `numactl`, which EasyBuild pulls in as **dependencies of `OpenMPI`** — so load `ml GCC OpenMPI` even for a pure-OpenMP job.
- The reference `pi_openmp.pbs` runs all three *before* the binary, so your `.o<jobid>` log will contain a topology block you can scroll through.

::: {.callout-tip}
You should be curious about the processor... details are important for efficient parallelization.
:::

## AMD Rome — the full node

![**Two sockets** joined by three inter-socket links (~96 GB/s theoretical, 63–72 GB/s sustained per direction). Each socket has 16 CCXes (4 cores + L3 each), 8 memory controllers driving 256 GB DDR4 at 204.8 GB/s, and a ×16 PCIe 4.0 link to the InfiniBand adapter. Source: NASA HECC.](images/rome_processor_v2.jpg){width="70%"}

## AMD Rome — sockets → CCDs → CCX

- Each socket holds **8 chiplets (CCDs)**; each CCD packs two **Core Complexes (CCX)**; each CCX = **4 cores + a shared 16 MB L3**.
- **2 sockets × 8 CCDs × 2 CCX × 4 cores = 128 cores per node.**
- Each socket's **I/O die** owns that socket's memory controllers; DRAM on the *other* socket is only reachable over the three inter-socket links.

## AMD Rome — cores, cache, NUMA

| Level | What you get |
|---|---|
| NUMA domains | **8** per node (4 per socket; each spans 2 CCDs) |
| L3 cache | 16 MB per CCX, shared by 4 cores |
| L2 / L1d | 512 KB / 32 KB per core |
| RAM | ~1 TB per node, split across the two sockets |

- **L3 is the sharing boundary.** Four cores in the same CCX share cache. Rome has no node-wide last-level cache — a 4-thread team that fits in one CCX performs very differently from one scattered across four.

::: {.callout-note}
The 4-NUMA-per-socket layout comes from the BIOS mode **NPS4** (NUMA-Per-Socket = 4). Each NUMA domain spans 2 CCDs — *not* one-per-CCX, which would give 16. CX3 Rome nodes are always in NPS4; `numactl --hardware` confirms.
:::


## AMD Rome — NUMA distances

From `numactl --hardware` on a Rome compute node:

```
          node 0   node 1 (same socket)   node 4 (other socket)
          ------   --------------------   ---------------------
distance:   10             12                      32
```

- SLIT values are **unitless BIOS hints**, not times — local is defined as 10 and everything else scales from that.
- Measured pointer-chase on `cx3-13-2`: **~104 ns local, ~120 ns same-socket, ~247 ns cross-socket** (≈2.4× local, not 3×). Full 8×8 matrix in the appendix.
- The inter-socket link carries *all* cross-socket memory traffic, so bandwidth is contended too.
- **Rule of thumb:** threads that share data → same NUMA domain; threads that don't → spread across sockets to use more memory controllers.

## Thread pinning

Without pinning, the kernel is free to move threads between cores — bad for caches, worse for NUMA. Two environment variables fix that:

```bash
export OMP_PLACES=cores          # smallest binding unit = one core
export OMP_PROC_BIND=close       # pack threads onto adjacent places
export OMP_DISPLAY_AFFINITY=TRUE # print one line per thread at startup
```

- `close` keeps threads packed (share a socket / NUMA node when possible). Good default for compute-bound kernels like our $\pi$.
- `spread` distributes threads across the machine. Use when you're **memory-bandwidth-bound** and want to hit multiple memory controllers.

**Reality check.** Asking for `ncpus=8` on a 128-core rome node does *not* guarantee contiguous cores. A real run of ours landed threads on cores `12, 30, 31, 47, 61, 62, 67, 79` — scattered across both sockets. `close` still pins them, but only *within* the cpuset PBS gave you. For true locality, request a whole NUMA domain (`ncpus=16`) or a whole socket.

## `OMP_NUM_THREADS`

- The `ompthreads` PBS directive is really just shorthand for:

  ```bash
  export OMP_NUM_THREADS=8
  ```

- You can set it yourself in the script if you prefer the long form.
- Without it, the OpenMP runtime chooses its own default from the visible CPUs — usually not what you want inside PBS.

## Hands-on — submit the OpenMP Pi

```bash
cd ic-hpc-intro/examples
qsub openmp/pi_openmp.pbs
qstat -u $USER
# when state = F:
cat pi_openmp.o*
```

Compare the wall-time to the serial run. With 8 threads expect roughly **6×–7×** speed-up (not 8× — launch, reduction, vectorisation, and CPU-frequency effects eat some).

## Reading the OpenMP log

`<jobname>.o<jobid>` file has two parts. First the topology block we set up earlier; confirm Rome node and to see which cores PBS alooocated. Secondly, the output of the run:

```bash
n=1000000000  threads=8  pi=3.141592653589821  time=0.163s
===============================================
CPU Time used: 00:01:18
CPU Percent:   789%
Memory usage:  18mb
Walltime:      00:00:01
```

- If PBS accounting reports `CPU Percent ≈ 800 %`, ~8 cores were active — good.
- Very short jobs can show unhelpful accounting such as `CPU Percent: 0%`; trust the program's `threads=...` line and the affinity lines first.
- If the program prints `threads=1`, OpenMP didn't activate (check `ompthreads`).
- The `.e<jobid>` file holds the `OMP_DISPLAY_AFFINITY` lines — one per thread, showing which core it pinned to.

## Scaling — what to expect

- Near-linear to **4–8 threads** for Pi.
- Flattens out as overheads, turbo-frequency changes, vector throughput, and socket placement dominate.
- Strong scaling on 128 threads will likely top out at a ~60–80× speed-up for this kernel on one Rome node.
- Real scientific codes rarely scale as well as Pi — this is the ceiling, not the floor.

## Common OpenMP pitfalls

- **Forgot `-fopenmp`** → pragmas silently ignored; program runs serially at full speed.
- **Forgot `reduction(+:sum)`** → data race; answer changes every run.
- **Declared loop index as shared** → threads fight over it; compile fails or answer is junk.
- **Wrote the inner-loop body as `sum = sum + …`** → reduction still works; but `sum = f(i)` overwrites instead of accumulates. Watch what you're summing.

# MPI on CX3

## Distributed memory parallelism

- MPI spreads a program across **many compute nodes** (and many cores within each).
- Each rank is a separate **process** with its own memory.
- To share data, ranks send explicit **messages** over the network.
- Scales far beyond OpenMP — thousands of cores routinely, $\sim$ a million at the high end.

## The SPMD model

- MPI = Message Passing Interface.
- "Single Program, Multiple Data":
  - You launch the *same executable* N times.
  - Each copy (rank) sees the same code but picks its own slice of the data using its rank ID.
- Your program branches on `rank == 0` / `rank == 1` / etc. to do different things.

## The six essentials

Most MPI programs need only these six calls:

```cpp
MPI_Init(&argc, &argv);
MPI_Comm_rank(MPI_COMM_WORLD, &rank);
MPI_Comm_size(MPI_COMM_WORLD, &size);

// ... do parallel work, exchange messages ...

MPI_Finalize();
```

Plus one of `MPI_Send` / `MPI_Recv` / `MPI_Reduce` / `MPI_Bcast` for data exchange.

## `MPI_Init` and `MPI_Finalize`

- `MPI_Init` must be called before any other MPI call.
- `MPI_Finalize` must be the last MPI call (per rank).
- Between them lives the entire parallel body of your program.
- Forgetting `MPI_Finalize` is legal but can hide resource leaks and confuse the launcher.

## Rank and size

```cpp
int rank, size;
MPI_Comm_rank(MPI_COMM_WORLD, &rank);  // 0 .. size-1
MPI_Comm_size(MPI_COMM_WORLD, &size);  // N

if (rank == 0) {
    // "master"-only work, e.g. reading config, writing results
}
```

- `MPI_COMM_WORLD` = "all ranks that were launched".
- Rank 0 is conventionally the *root* for reductions / I/O.

## Collective communication — `MPI_Reduce`

```cpp
double local  = /* this rank's partial sum */;
double global = 0.0;

MPI_Reduce(&local, &global, 1, MPI_DOUBLE, MPI_SUM,
           /*root=*/0, MPI_COMM_WORLD);

if (rank == 0) {
    std::cout << std::setprecision(15) << "global sum = " << global << '\n';
}
```

- Every rank calls `MPI_Reduce` with its local value.
- MPI combines them (here, sum) and delivers the result to the **root** rank only.
- One function call; the network communication is hidden.

## Pi — MPI version

```{=latex}
\scriptsize                                                                                                                                                                   
```   

```cpp
MPI_Init(&argc, &argv);
int rank, size;
MPI_Comm_rank(MPI_COMM_WORLD, &rank);
MPI_Comm_size(MPI_COMM_WORLD, &size);

const long long n = 1'000'000'000LL;
const long long chunk  = n / size;
const long long start  = 1 + rank * chunk;
const long long finish = (rank == size - 1) ? n : (rank + 1) * chunk;

double local_sum = 0.0;
for (long long i = start; i <= finish; ++i) {
    const double x = (1.0 / n) * (i - 0.5);
    local_sum += 4.0 / (1.0 + x * x);
}

double global_sum = 0.0;
MPI_Reduce(&local_sum, &global_sum, 1, MPI_DOUBLE, MPI_SUM, 0, MPI_COMM_WORLD);
if (rank == 0) std::cout << std::setprecision(15) << "pi = " << (global_sum / n) << '\n';
MPI_Finalize();
```

```{=latex}                                                                                                                                                                   
\normalsize     
```

## Building — `mpicxx`

Command-line:

```bash
ml tools/prod GCC OpenMPI
mpicxx -O3 pi_mpi.cpp -o pi_mpi
```

`mpicxx` is a compiler wrapper — it calls `g++` with the MPI include + link flags for you. CMake:

```cmake
find_package(MPI REQUIRED COMPONENTS CXX)
add_executable(pi_mpi pi_mpi.cpp)
target_link_libraries(pi_mpi PRIVATE MPI::MPI_CXX)
```

You do **not** call `mpicxx` yourself when using CMake.

## PBS for MPI — `mpiprocs`

```bash
#!/bin/bash
#PBS -N pi_mpi
#PBS -l walltime=00:10:00
#PBS -l select=1:ncpus=16:mem=4gb:mpiprocs=16:cpu_type=rome

cd $PBS_O_WORKDIR
ml tools/prod
ml GCC OpenMPI

mpiexec ./build/mpi/pi_mpi
```

- `mpiprocs=16` — 16 MPI ranks on this chunk.
- `mpiexec` (no `-n` needed) — PBS tells it how many ranks.

## Single node vs. multi-node

* Single node, 16 ranks — fast to schedule:
```bash
#PBS -l select=1:ncpus=16:mem=4gb:mpiprocs=16:cpu_type=rome

* Two nodes, 64 ranks each = 128 ranks total — bigger but queues longer:
```bash
#PBS -l select=2:ncpus=64:mem=64gb:mpiprocs=64:cpu_type=rome
```

- Rule of thumb for an intro demo: stay on 1 node.
- Multi-node routes through the `v1_capability*` queues, which have heavier backlogs.
- MPI within a single node still goes over shared memory — fast.

## Running — `mpiexec`

- Always use `mpiexec` (not `./pi_mpi`) to launch an MPI program.
- Inside PBS you don't pass `-n`; PBS exposes the rank count via its hostfile.
- Locally on your laptop: `mpiexec -n 4 ./pi_mpi` — the `-n` is explicit.
- The executable must be in a path visible to every rank — `$HOME` / RDS satisfies this.

## Hands-on — submit the MPI Pi

```bash
cd ic-hpc-intro/examples
qsub mpi/pi_mpi.pbs
qstat -u $USER
# when state = F:
cat pi_mpi.o*
```

Edit the `.pbs` file, change `mpiprocs=16` to `4`, 8, 32 in turn, and plot the wall-time against rank count. You should see near-linear scaling up to ~32 ranks, then flattening.

## Reading the MPI log

Typical output:

```bash
n=1000000000  ranks=16  pi=3.141592653589793  time=0.076s
===============================================
CPU Time used: 00:01:13
CPU Percent:   1581%
Memory usage:  52mb
```

- If PBS accounting reports `CPU Percent ≈ 16 × 100 %`, all ranks were busy.
- Very short MPI jobs can also show weak accounting data; trust the program's `ranks=...` line first.
- If only one rank prints, rank 0 is doing its job (the others stay silent).

## Scaling across nodes

- Within a single node: MPI uses **shared memory** under the hood → very fast.
- Across nodes: messages go over the **InfiniBand interconnect** → still fast, but higher latency per call.
- Pi has a single `MPI_Reduce` per run — the cost is negligible.
- Real codes with frequent small messages scale worse; aim to pack communication.

## Common MPI pitfalls

- **`mpiexec: command not found`** → forgot `ml OpenMPI`.
- **Mismatched `ncpus` and `mpiprocs`** → PBS will let you over-subscribe; don't.
- **Rank 0 reads a file, others try to read it too** → race on the filesystem; either have only rank 0 read and `MPI_Bcast`, or accept repeated reads.
- **`MPI_Reduce` called from only some ranks** → deadlock. *Every* rank in the communicator must call every collective.
- **Forgetting `MPI_Finalize`** → job doesn't crash but leaves resources hanging.

# Wrap-up

## OpenMP vs. MPI — when to use which

| Question | OpenMP | MPI |
|---|---|---|
| Fits in one node's RAM? | yes | either |
| Do I want to start with one pragma? | yes | no |
| Need >128 cores? | no | yes |
| Shared-memory data structures? | yes | no (explicit messages) |
| Debugging difficulty | moderate | harder |

Rule of thumb: **OpenMP first, MPI when you outgrow one node**.

## Hybrid MPI + OpenMP (teaser)

```bash
#PBS -l select=2:ncpus=64:mem=64gb:mpiprocs=1:ompthreads=64
```

- One MPI rank per node × 64 OpenMP threads per rank.
- Saves memory (one copy of the data per node, not per core).
- Reduces MPI message count.

## Where to go next

- **OpenMP**: Remainder of this week we are focused on OpenMP
- **MPI**: Following two weeks focus on MPI.
- **CX3 docs**: <https://icl-rcs-user-guide.readthedocs.io/>
- **Queue picker**: <https://icl-rcs-user-guide.readthedocs.io/en/latest/hpc/queues/job-sizing-guidance/>
- **Self-service**: <https://selfservice.rcs.imperial.ac.uk/> — see your jobs in a prettier qstat.

## Help and support

- **Me** — g.gorman@imperial.ac.uk. Bring: job id, PBS script, `.o` / `.e` logs, a one-line description of what you expected.
- **RCS ASK form** — <https://www.imperial.ac.uk/admin-services/ict/self-service/research-support/rcs/get-support/contact-us/>
- **Weekly drop-in clinic** — <https://icl-rcs-user-guide.readthedocs.io/en/latest/support/attend-a-clinic/>

## Summary

Today you should leave with:

- An SSH session open to CX3.
- A compiled `pi_serial`, `pi_openmp`, `pi_mpi`.
- Three `.pbs` log files with $\pi = 3.14159265…$ in them.
- An intuition for when to reach for OpenMP vs MPI.

Everything else is practice.

## Questions?

::: {.center}
**Thank you — and happy computing!**
:::

# Appendix

## Measured NUMA latencies on a Rome node

From `examples/benchmarks/numa_latency/` on **`cx3-13-2`** (EPYC 7742, NPS4): 256 MB pointer-chase (>> L3), single-cycle permutation, 50M dereferences per cell. CPU pinned with `numactl --cpunodebind`, memory bound via `numa_alloc_onnode`.

ns per DRAM access:

```{=latex}
\tiny                                                                                                                                                                   
```
| src\\tgt |   0 |   1 |   2 |   3 |   4 |   5 |   6 |   7 |
|:---:|----:|----:|----:|----:|----:|----:|----:|----:|
| **0** | **104** | 117 | 126 | 129 | 250 | 249 | 246 | 244 |
| **1** | 116 | **104** | 129 | 125 | 251 | 253 | 249 | 248 |
| **2** | 123 | 129 | **104** | 117 | 243 | 243 | 246 | 247 |
| **3** | 128 | 124 | 118 | **103** | 249 | 250 | 251 | 252 |
| **4** | 246 | 249 | 243 | 245 | **103** | 117 | 125 | 128 |
| **5** | 249 | 250 | 249 | 249 | 116 | **105** | 128 | 123 |
| **6** | 243 | 244 | 247 | 247 | 125 | 129 | **104** | 116 |
| **7** | 246 | 248 | 251 | 252 | 129 | 125 | 118 | **104** |
```{=latex}
\normalsize      
```

## NUMA latencies on a Rome node - summary

- **Local** (diagonal): ~104 ns
- **Same-socket, adjacent NUMA** (0↔1, 2↔3, 4↔5, 6↔7): ~117 ns
- **Same-socket, non-adjacent**: ~126 ns
- **Cross-socket** (via xGMI): ~247 ns

SLIT distances 10 / 12 / 32 → measured ratios **1.00 / 1.12–1.24 / 2.37**. SLIT orders the tiers correctly but overstates cross-socket (~2.4× not 3.2×) and hides the ~10% intra-socket structure (same SLIT "12" covers both 117 and 129 ns pairs).
